interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Second Chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC); Orangeburg, SC student movement leader
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill (on behalf of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture)

main_summary.summary:
Charles F. McDew, the second chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), recounts his journey from a steel-and-football town in Ohio to the leadership of one of the most consequential student movements of the twentieth century. Born June 23, 1938 in Massillon, Ohio, McDew grew up in a unionized industrial community whose Black families included his father (who played for Paul Brown's Massillon Tigers and trained as a teacher but could not be hired in northern schools before the 1954 Brown v. Board decision), his grandfather and uncles who worked the steel mills, and his uncle Horace Gillom, a punter and end for the Cleveland Browns. He describes Massillon as an immigrant working-town in which racial politics arrived from the outside through football integration battles such as the campaign supporting Bobby Grier, the University of Pittsburgh player whom Orange Bowl organizers tried to bar.

McDew's life pivoted when his father, a South Carolina State College graduate, insisted he attend a historically Black college for at least one year. Sent to Orangeburg, South Carolina in the fall of 1959, McDew encountered legal segregation directly for the first time during the drive South and then, dramatically, during a Thanksgiving visit to Sumter, South Carolina, when local police beat him after he failed to address an officer with the required deference, breaking his arm and his jaw. Within roughly twenty-four hours he was arrested three times in succession -- once for the fight, once for refusing to ride in the segregated baggage car, and once for cutting through Edisto Park on a day Black people were not permitted to be there. After the February 1, 1960 sit-in by North Carolina A&T students in Greensboro, fellow students approached McDew, by then known on campus for speaking to white people 'just like regular people,' and asked him to lead a sit-in movement in Orangeburg.

McDew became the head of the Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement and was arrested in Columbia after attempting to deliver a petition to Governor Ernest Hollings; he was one of roughly 800 students arrested in a single Orangeburg desegregation petition action. In April 1960 he attended the meeting at Shaw University in Raleigh, called by Dr. Martin Luther King Jr. and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference, which produced SNCC. McDew describes refusing to accept King's proposal that the new group simply join SCLC -- he respected nonviolence as a tactic but would not pledge it as a way of life, arguing that what worked for Gandhi in India would not protect protesters in the American South. He and others convened down the hall and founded what they first called the student coordinating committee, then named the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee at the urging of James Lawson's Nashville students, whose presence McDew valued in part because Lawson had been the minister at his mother's St. James AME church in Massillon.

Marion Barry became SNCC's first chairman; McDew succeeded him. He recounts the Rock Hill, South Carolina jail-no-bail program and trips to Mississippi, where SNCC chose voter registration alongside direct action because they had concluded that long-tenured Southern senators -- Stennis, Eastland, Byrd, Talmadge -- drew their power from Black-majority counties where Black people did not vote, and that breaking that arrangement could 'change this country in a short period of time.' He closes with reflections on the importance of education, on the Posse Foundation, on Mississippi's required civil-rights curriculum, on the Children's Defense Fund, and on the Smithsonian museum then being built, insisting that the struggle continues and that the soul of the nation will be strengthened by understanding SNCC's philosophy and the broader civil-rights movement.
key_themes: Black northern industrial childhood and union culture
First encounter with Jim Crow segregation
Police violence and the limits of nonviolence as personal creed
Student-led origins of SNCC and the April 1960 Shaw University meeting
Voter registration as long-game political strategy in the Black Belt
Generational pledge to accept the 'blood price' of the movement
main_summary.historical_significance: McDew's interview is a foundational firsthand account of SNCC's origin, naming, and strategic identity, given by the organization's second chairman. He preserves the internal debate at Shaw University over whether nonviolence would be a tactic or a way of life, the rationale behind targeting Black Belt voter registration as a structural attack on segregationist Senate power, and the early Orangeburg movement's mass-arrest action, all of which are central to scholarship on the youth wing of the civil rights movement. His Northern industrial-union origin story also complicates the still-common framing of civil-rights activism as exclusively Southern-born.

errors: The summary states 'February 1, 1960 sit-in by North Carolina A&T students in Greensboro' as historical fact. The transcript at segment 163 actually says 'February 1, 1964' -- which is internally inconsistent with surrounding chronology (McDew has just described arriving at school in fall 1959 and being told by his father at Christmas to come home). The summary silently corrects the speaker's date to the historically accurate 1960 rather than reporting what the transcript says verbatim. This is a faithful-to-history but unfaithful-to-transcript choice and is being flagged.

The summary names 'St. James AME church in Massillon' as the church where James Lawson was minister to McDew's mother. The transcript at segment 50 says 'St. James' AME Church' and identifies Lawson as the minister of McDew's mother's church, but the transcript does not explicitly place that church in Massillon -- it is inferred from the surrounding narrative about Massillon. Marked as a contextual inference, not a direct transcript claim.
The summary describes Horace Gillom as 'a punter and end for the Cleveland Browns.' The transcript at segment 52 says 'an end and a punter for the Cleveland Browns,' which matches.
improvements: Either preserve the speaker's stated date ('February 1, 1964') with a parenthetical correction, or remove the specific date and use 'after the February 1960 Greensboro sit-in' phrasing that is supported by the broader chronology without reproducing the speaker's evident misspeak.
Qualify the 'St. James AME in Massillon' phrasing as 'his mother's St. James AME church' to avoid asserting a location the transcript does not directly state.
Consider naming Rev. C.A. Ivory and I. DeQuincey Newman explicitly in the Rock Hill paragraph since the speaker identifies them as central.

chapters.title: Massillon, Ohio: A Steel Town of Football Players and Union Workers
chapters.summary: McDew opens with his birth in Massillon, Ohio on June 23, 1938 and sketches the town's character: about 35,000-38,000 people, known for steel production and high-school football. He describes the Massillon Tigers football tradition associated with Paul Brown -- both McDew's father and grandfather played for the Tigers -- and the proximity of the Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton, eight miles away, with its long Massillon-Canton rivalry. His father worked at Republic Steel; the two largest employers in town were Republic Steel and Massillon Steel Casting. McDew notes Massillon's place in U.S. labor history, naming Ben Fischer (rendered in the transcript as 'Ben Ferris'), president of the United Steelworkers, and Jacob Coxey ('General Coxey') of Coxey's Army of the unemployed, both Massillon figures.
related_events: Pro Football Hall of Fame in Canton

errors: The transcript renders the steelworkers president as 'Ben Ferris'; the canonical name in U.S. labor history is Ben Fischer. The summary glosses this rather than preserving the transcript spelling. Marked as a soft transcription correction.
improvements: Consider footnoting the transcription variant for 'Ben Ferris/Fischer' rather than silently normalizing.

chapters.title: Integrated Schools, Football, and Early Race Lessons in Massillon
chapters.summary:
Asked about schools, McDew explains that Massillon's grade schools were neighborhood schools and the city had three junior highs feeding one high school, so 'you'd go to school with the same kids I went to the first grade with.' He had never attended a Black school until he went South. He notes that his sister was one of the first Black teachers in the Massillon public schools and that his father, trained as a teacher, could not be hired in northern schools before the 1954 Brown v. Board decision because 'there just weren't many if any black teachers in northern schools.' He estimates Massillon's roughly 3,500 Black residents within a population of 38,000.

Asked whether he was involved in civil-rights activity before going South, McDew recalls supporting Bobby Grier, the Black University of Pittsburgh football player whom Orange Bowl organizers tried to bar from a game against a team with Black players, and traveling about 120 miles from Massillon to Pittsburgh as a child to back him. He also recalls writing letters of support to James Lawson when Lawson was jailed as a conscientious objector, and identifies Lawson as the minister at his mother's St. James AME church and as 'a advisor to Dr. King' whom he credits with introducing nonviolence to the American people. His uncle Horace Gillom, an end and punter for the Cleveland Browns, brought football's racial controversies into the household.
related_events: Bobby Grier / 1956 Sugar Bowl integration controversy (referenced as 'Orange Bowl' by speaker)

errors: McDew states Bobby Grier was barred from an 'Orange Bowl' game. Grier's actual bowl game was the 1956 Sugar Bowl. The summary preserves the speaker's word 'Orange Bowl' rather than correcting it, which is faithful to the transcript but historically inaccurate. Flagged for transparency.
improvements: Add a parenthetical 'Sugar Bowl, per historical record' next to the 'Orange Bowl' phrasing if a verified historical anchor is desired without overwriting the speaker.

chapters.title: Lessons in Economics and the Working-Class Immigrant City
chapters.summary: McDew describes Massillon as 'an expression of the American dream' built by immigrant steelworkers from Greece, Spain, Yugoslavia, Czechoslovakia, and the American South -- noting that Southerners coming north were as much immigrants to him as the European-born workers. He recalls hearing the elderly General Jacob Coxey, still alive, and the old men of his grandfather's generation -- veterans of Coxey's march on Washington with 'the army of the poor' -- talking history in the barbershop. He explains how he absorbed economics by watching the supply-and-demand cycle of steel: ore mined in Minnesota at the Mesabi Iron Range came to Massillon to be made into steel, the steel went to Detroit and Chicago to be turned into automobiles, and the cycle alternated between full-employment overtime and layoffs in which families had to 'pick up coal, falling off of trains to heat your homes.' He also describes his mother's training as a nurse at Homer G. Phillips in St. Louis (one of only two cities, with Chicago, where Black women could train as nurses at the time) and her becoming one of the first Black nurses hired at the local hospital after World War II.
related_events: Homer G. Phillips Hospital nursing school

improvements: The transcript at segment 75 says 'one was in Chicago, one was in St. Louis' for nursing schools. The summary phrases this as 'one of only two cities... where Black women could train as nurses at the time' -- this is a slight paraphrase widening that the speaker may not have intended. Consider tightening.

chapters.title: Father Sends Him South: First Encounter with Jim Crow
chapters.summary:
McDew explains that he had planned to play football at Michigan, go pro, marry, and retire -- 'set' by ninth grade. But his father, a graduate of South Carolina State College and a former chemistry teacher in South Carolina, insisted that all his children spend at least one year at a historically Black college or university. The father's purpose was for McDew to see a Black professional class beyond athletes and preachers. McDew arrived at South Carolina State in Orangeburg in the fall of 1959, having never been South before.

The drive from Massillon to Orangeburg on U.S. Route 21 -- which begins in Cleveland and ends at the South Carolina coast -- was McDew's introduction to segregation. His father warned him to use the bathroom in Ohio because 'once we leave Ohio and we're in West Virginia, we can't use these facilities,' and his mother packed lunches because food could not be bought along the way. McDew recalls that by the time the family reached Bluefield, West Virginia, segregation was inescapable. On arrival in Orangeburg, McDew was struck by the campus's many young Black women -- 'thousands, hundreds... in one day, and I had seen in my entire life' -- to whom, unlike in Massillon, he was not related, an observation he frames with both humor and the genuine relief of finding a Black social world he had never known.

improvements: Could include McDew's father's name if it surfaced; it does not in this transcript, so leaving it unattributed is correct.

chapters.title: Sumter Arrest: Three Arrests in Two Days
chapters.summary:
Over Thanksgiving 1959, McDew accepted an invitation from his roommate Charles Gatson to spend the holiday at Gatson's home in Sumter, South Carolina. After a party that night, McDew -- the designated driver because he did not drink -- was pulled over by police. When the officer asked, 'Where are you from, boy?,' McDew handed over his Ohio license, prompting the officer to ask, 'Didn't they ever teach you how to say yes sir, no sir to white men up there?' and to strike him. McDew, from 'a tough steel town,' fought back, was beaten by the officer's partner, and was arrested for the first time in his life on charges of disturbing the peace and disobeying an officer; the police broke his arm and his jaw.

Hospitalized and then released, McDew tried to board a train back to Orangeburg and refused to ride in the segregated 'baggage car' the conductor directed him to, sitting instead in the white car -- and was arrested a second time for violating segregation laws. Back in Orangeburg, he cut through Edisto Park to reach campus and was arrested a third time, since Black people were permitted in the public park only on two specified days. Three arrests in roughly two days. McDew describes learning the catalogue of segregation laws including the prohibition on a Black person looking a white woman in the face. By Christmas his father, having paid the medical bills and bonds, was telling him to come home; McDew agreed.

improvements: Could quote McDew's 'I don't do baggage cars' verbatim for vividness.

chapters.title: Greensboro Sit-ins and Becoming Spokesperson at Orangeburg
chapters.summary: McDew describes the Greensboro sit-in by students from North Carolina A&T as the moment that pulled him into organized civil-rights work. The transcript dates the Greensboro action 'February 1, 1964,' but the surrounding chronology (McDew is still a freshman in his first year, fall 1959 to early 1960) makes clear he means the historically documented February 1, 1960 sit-in. After Greensboro, a group of students approached McDew at South Carolina State and asked him to be their spokesperson for a sit-in movement at Orangeburg. McDew explains that he had a campus reputation for being 'unusual' in that he would 'speak to white people... just like regular people,' without deference or fear, which made students want him as their voice. His initial answer was no -- he expected to be on a train home to Ohio in a few weeks and never to come further south again than Columbus, Ohio for the annual Michigan-Ohio State game.

errors: The transcript's spoken date 'February 1, 1964' is acknowledged in the chapter summary as a probable misspeak rather than reproduced as a fact. The Greensboro sit-ins are historically documented as February 1, 1960. Flagged because the chapter explicitly tells the reader about the inconsistency.
improvements: Acceptable as written; the inconsistency is disclosed rather than silently corrected.

chapters.title: The Shaw University Meeting and the Founding of SNCC
chapters.summary:
McDew became head of the Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement and the student leader of the Orangeburg sit-ins, weight-ins, and other direct-action tests. He was arrested in Columbia, South Carolina after attempting to deliver a petition to Governor Ernest 'Fritz' Hollings -- Black people were permitted at the State Capitol only on Sundays between four and six -- and on another occasion was one of roughly 800 students arrested in an Orangeburg desegregation petition action.

A letter from Dr. King and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference invited students to a meeting at Shaw University in Raleigh, North Carolina in April 1960. McDew attended as the South Carolina State representative. At Shaw, Dr. King proposed that all the student groups join SCLC and that members pledge nonviolence as a way of life. McDew refused: he respected nonviolence as a tactic but argued that what worked for Gandhi in India would fail in the American South, where 'a group of black people lay down on railroad tracks here in South Carolina, Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, Texas, Louisiana, any of these southern states, a train would run you over a backup to make certain you are dead.' Those who would not pledge nonviolence as a life philosophy convened down the hall. Initially they considered calling themselves the student coordinating committee. They added 'nonviolent' to the name to include the Nashville students -- trained by James Lawson -- who deeply believed in the philosophy. That meeting gave SNCC its name and its founding identity.
related_events: Shaw University founding meeting of SNCC (April 1960)

errors: McDew at segment 190 says 'when Gandhi tried nonviolence in South Africa, it was beaten, jailed, and run out of the country.' The summary does not reproduce this specific claim. Historically Gandhi did spend years in South Africa, was arrested, and left, but the framing 'run out of the country' is the speaker's; the summary collapses this to the more general Gandhi-in-India contrast. Flagged because the South Africa reference was specifically made by the speaker.
improvements: Add a sentence preserving McDew's specific Gandhi-in-South-Africa claim if a more complete account of his philosophical critique is desired.

chapters.title: SNCC Chair, Party Conventions, and Rock Hill Jail-No-Bail
chapters.summary:
McDew describes SNCC's first organizational moves after the Shaw meeting: aiming to address both major-party national conventions that summer (one or both, he recalls, in Los Angeles) to ask for a civil-rights plank, and electing Marion Barry as SNCC's first chairman to carry that work. In the fall, with SCLC office space and seed support, the organization built its structure. McDew explains how the name 'SNCC' began to be pronounced 'Snick' through reporter usage -- a journalist somewhere just taking the letters and reading them.

McDew succeeded Barry as chairman. He then recounts the Rock Hill, South Carolina jail-no-bail program, the first test of the tactic SNCC had been developing in response to the realization that they could not keep operating if they kept bailing out: 'we cannot get out' was reframed as 'we will stay in.' McDew identifies Rev. C.A. Ivory of Rock Hill and I. DeQuincey Newman, then the head of the South Carolina NAACP, as central local figures. Rock Hill became the proving ground for the jail-no-bail concept, with SNCC people specifically going to be arrested and refusing to post bond.
related_events: Marion Barry as first SNCC chair (1960)
Rock Hill, South Carolina jail-no-bail action

errors: The 1960 Democratic National Convention was in Los Angeles; the Republican National Convention was in Chicago. McDew says 'one of them or both of them were in Los Angeles' -- the summary preserves this uncertainty rather than asserting one or the other. Flagged because the summary does not supply the historically known answer (Democrats in LA, Republicans in Chicago).

The summary repeats McDew's account that the 'Snick' pronunciation came from a reporter. This is a specific causal attribution that the transcript supports but that may not be the only or earliest origin documented in the historical record. Marked as speaker-attributed.
improvements: If a fact-check is in scope, add a clarifying parenthetical that the 1960 Democratic Convention was in Los Angeles and the 1960 Republican Convention was in Chicago.

chapters.title: Mississippi, Voter Registration, and the Black Belt Strategy
chapters.summary:
By the time SNCC went to Mississippi, McDew says, the organization had its own personality -- it was SNCC, not CORE. After the Freedom Rides, SNCC moved into voter registration, going first to McComb in Southwest Mississippi. McDew describes the two-track program SNCC settled on: direct action (sit-ins, weight-ins) and voter registration. He places the voter-registration choice inside SNCC's broader strategic self-understanding. SNCC, he says, was planned with a finite five-year life, partly because students had to give their parents a concrete answer -- 'I have to do this, this is what I'm going to do... for five years' -- and partly because the founders judged that within five years members would be 'in jail, insane, or incarcerated.'

McDew then explains the structural rationale for choosing the Black Belt. SNCC analyzed where real power in the federal government lay -- not the presidency but the House and Senate, where seniority created committee chairs. Those chairs were held by white Southern men from heavily Black counties in Louisiana, Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, Texas, places where Black majorities did not vote out of disinterest, fear, or violent retaliation. He names Dougherty, Terrell, and Baker counties in Southwest Georgia and Amite, Pike, and Walthall counties in Southwest Mississippi as targets. He invokes James Eastland, John Stennis, Robert Byrd, and Herman Talmadge as the kind of long-tenured senators whose power flowed from this arrangement. The strategic conclusion was simple: register Black voters in the worst counties and break the seniority of segregationist senators.

McDew closes the chapter with SNCC's collective pledge to accept the violence that registration work would bring. They knew, he says, that they would be 'beaten, bombed, or killed,' and they pledged that none of those outcomes would turn them back. He calls the United States 'the most violent country on the face of the earth' and frames the registration campaign as a 'blood price' the movement had decided to pay.

errors: The transcript's audio appears to have inserted 'Byrd' alongside 'Talmadge' (segment 281). Robert Byrd was the West Virginia senator; he was a former Klan member but not a Southern Black Belt segregationist of the Eastland/Stennis type. The summary preserves McDew's list as spoken (Stennis, Eastland, Byrd, Talmadge) rather than adjusting it; flagged because some readers may want clarification that Byrd's relationship to Southern Black Belt power was different in kind from Eastland's and Stennis's.
improvements: If editorial annotation is in scope, note parenthetically that the canonical Black Belt segregationist senators included Eastland (MS), Stennis (MS), Russell (GA), and Talmadge (GA); Byrd (WV) is a different category.

chapters.title: Reflections on Education, the Posse Foundation, and Continuing the Struggle
chapters.summary:
Asked for closing thoughts, McDew identifies education as the key to the future of Black America. He describes a recent visit to Colby College in Maine to speak to the Posse Foundation (rendered in the transcript as 'Posse Foundation Foundation,' a stutter or transcription artifact), a scholarship program that places cohorts of Black and Hispanic students at top universities -- Harvard among them. McDew says Colby was the first school outside the South in 45 years at which he had spoken to an entirely Black American student audience, and he was struck and moved by it.

He approves of President Obama having directed a portion of his Nobel Peace Prize proceeds to the Posse Foundation. He praises Mississippi's requirement that students pass a course in civil rights before finishing college and argues every state should do the same. He emphasizes that 'the miseducation of our children has to be corrected' -- that students at Black colleges today often know little about the SNCC and movement work that made their attendance possible. He praises the Children's Defense Fund (founded by Marian Wright Edelman) and the Smithsonian's planned museum, then in construction. He closes by saying that 'the struggle continues. It is not over,' and that the soul of the nation will be cleansed and strengthened by an honest reckoning with the philosophy of SNCC and the civil-rights struggle.
related_events: Barack Obama Nobel Peace Prize donation
Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

errors: The summary attributes the Children's Defense Fund founding to Marian Wright Edelman, which is historically correct, but McDew does not name her in the transcript -- he says only 'the children defense fund.' Marked as a corpus-confirmed contextual addition rather than a transcript-supported claim.
improvements: Could remove the parenthetical attribution to Edelman to be strictly transcript-faithful, since the speaker does not name her.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: Second Chairman of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Opens with chronological scene-setting in Massillon (Segment 9, 00:01:13,000): 'I was born in Massillon, Ohio, June 23, 1938.'

Smooth pivot from northern childhood to South via father's mandate (Segment 87-94, 00:20:18,000-00:22:21,000): 'I had to get South Carolina State... my father felt that we... should all go to a historically black college.'
Closes with reflective coda on education and continuing struggle (Segment 336, 01:18:17,000): 'But the struggle continues. It is not over.'
story_structure_coherence.justification: The interview follows a strong chronological arc -- childhood, college, awakening, SNCC founding, leadership, and reflection. Mosnier's pacing questions ('I'm watching the clock') keep the narrator on track. Transitions between Massillon, Sumter, Orangeburg, Raleigh, Rock Hill, and Mississippi are clear, and the closing reflection is the kind of synthetic resolution this rubric rewards.

most_vivid_moment.quote: When I said, before he hit me, he said, didn't he never teach you how to say yes sir, no sort of white men up there? I said, you got to be kidding. Yes sir, no sort of white men. Why did you say I do that? And he hit me.

evidence: Reconstructed dialogue with the Sumter officer (Segment 129-135, 00:33:29,000-00:34:46,000): 'where are you from, boy?' / 'didn't they ever teach you how to say yes sir, no sort of white men up there?'
Specific count and location: 'about 800 of us were arrested' in the Orangeburg petition action (Segment 182, 00:44:49,000)
Concrete economic chain: ore from Mesabi to steel in Massillon to cars in Detroit (Segment 78-80, 00:17:27,000-00:18:14,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: McDew offers rich reconstructed dialogue at moments of confrontation (the Sumter cop, the train conductor), precise dates and counts (June 23, 1938; ~800 arrests; 120 miles to Pittsburgh), and named geography (Bluefield, Edisto Park, Route 21). Some passages of philosophical reflection lean more abstract, which keeps this just below the 9-10 band.

most_authentic_moment.quote: And the cops beat me bloody, broke my arm, busted my jaw.

vulnerability_elements: Admits his original life plan was football, money, and 'a good looking woman' -- not activism (Segment 90, 00:21:03,000)
Acknowledges fear of the cost of the work: 'in five years, you'd be in jail, insane, or incarcerated' (Segment 261, 01:03:32,000)
Names his father's worry that the South would kill him and his own willingness to come home (Segment 159-160, 00:39:44,000-00:39:58,000)
emotional_authenticity.justification: McDew alternates humor (the social-life punchline on arriving at Orangeburg) with hard candor about being beaten by police and about the founders' acceptance that some of them would die. He does not perform martyrdom -- he describes the fear and the bargain calmly. The blend is unusual and authentic.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: McDew is a structured, vivid, and emotionally direct narrator. The arc from Northern industrial childhood to SNCC chairmanship is told with both reconstructed dialogue and analytic clarity. He balances reportorial precision (dates, counts, place names) with moments of disarming personal honesty. The narrative loses some momentum in the final reflective chapter, where rhetoric occasionally overtakes specific anecdote, but the core arc is exceptional.

major_events: April 1960 Shaw University meeting that founded SNCC -- McDew was the South Carolina State representative present (Segment 184, 00:45:17,000)
Orangeburg petition action with roughly 800 arrests (Segment 182, 00:44:49,000)
Refusal to join SCLC under Dr. King's terms at Shaw (Segment 186-194, 00:46:06,000-00:48:31,000)
Rock Hill, South Carolina jail-no-bail program (Segment 224-227, 00:57:03,000-00:58:25,000)

key_figures.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Convening figure at Shaw meeting whose proposal McDew refused

key_figures.name: Marion Barry
key_figures.relationship: First SNCC chairman whom McDew succeeded

key_figures.name: James Lawson
key_figures.relationship: Family minister at McDew's mother's St. James AME church

key_figures.name: I. DeQuincey Newman
key_figures.relationship: Head of South Carolina NAACP, allied figure

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: McDew preserves the internal debate at the Shaw meeting that produced SNCC, including the specific objection to joining SCLC and the reason 'nonviolent' was added to the organization's name. He is one of very few people who can give a firsthand account of the room where SNCC was named.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: McDew's vantage on the founding of SNCC is unmatched: he is the second chairman and was present at Shaw, and he articulates the strategic rationale for the Black Belt voter-registration choice that became SNCC's defining geography. This is canonical movement testimony.

precision_examples: Fall 1959 arrival at South Carolina State (Segment 100, 00:24:42,000)
Roughly 800 students arrested in Orangeburg petition action (Segment 182, 00:44:49,000)
Specific counties named: Dougherty, Terrell, Baker (GA); Amite, Pike, Walthall (MS) (Segments 275-276, 01:06:05,000-01:06:24,000)
verifiable_claims: April 1960 Shaw University meeting (verifiable, matches historical record)
Marion Barry as first SNCC chairman (verifiable, matches civil_rights_facts.json)
McDew as second SNCC chairman (verifiable, matches civil_rights_facts.json)
Greensboro sit-in date 'February 1' (1960 not 1964 -- speaker error)
I. DeQuincey Newman as head of South Carolina NAACP (verifiable)
Horace Gillom played end and punter for the Cleveland Browns (verifiable)
specificity_of_details.justification: Most named figures and organizations are verifiable. McDew's stated 'February 1, 1964' for Greensboro is the one clear factual misspeak; the broader chronology makes 1960 obvious. The interview is otherwise unusually precise about places, counties, organizational structure, and personal genealogy.

new_information: Strategic rationale for choosing Black Belt counties as voter-registration targets, articulated as a calculated attack on Senate seniority (Segments 263-294, 01:04:02,000-01:09:21,000)
The 'five-year planned death' rationale for SNCC, partly because students had to give parents a concrete time horizon (Segments 249-261, 01:01:57,000-01:03:32,000)
Why 'nonviolent' was added to SNCC's name: to keep the Nashville Lawson-trained students in the coalition (Segments 197-199, 00:49:23,000-00:50:01,000)
Northern Black industrial-union childhood as preparation for civil rights work, complicating the South-born-activist framing
understudied_aspects: Massillon, Ohio as a Black industrial-union community
Northern HBCU sending pipeline ('father wanted me to see a professional class of Black people')
The internal Shaw debate over nonviolence as tactic versus way of life
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Complicates the conventional narrative that SNCC was uniformly committed to nonviolence as philosophy: McDew makes clear that for many founders nonviolence was strictly tactical, and that the addition of 'nonviolent' to the name was a coalitional accommodation to the Nashville faction.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Useful as a primary source for any scholarship on the founding moments of SNCC, on student leadership styles before and after the lunch-counter movement, and on the strategic theory behind targeting Black Belt voter registration as a structural attack on segregationist federal power.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: McDew's interview contributes substantively to historiography of SNCC's founding identity and its strategic theory. The Black-Belt-Senate-seniority analysis is articulated with unusual clarity for a personal oral history.

historical_value.overall_assessment: Among the most historically valuable interviews in the collection. McDew is the second SNCC chairman recounting the founding of the organization from inside the room, naming local Rock Hill and Orangeburg figures who otherwise rarely surface in national accounts, and articulating the strategic theory behind the Black Belt voter-registration choice. One date misspeak (Greensboro stated as 1964) is the only meaningful factual blemish.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: From a Northern teenager whose life plan was football, marriage, and a liquor store, to the second chairman of SNCC and an advocate of confrontational voter-registration strategy in the Deep South. The pivot is the Sumter beating and the cascade of three arrests in two days.
key_turning_points: Refusing to ride the baggage car (Segment 147-150, 00:37:13,000-00:37:50,000)
Agreeing to be Orangeburg students' spokesperson after Greensboro (Segments 165-172, 00:40:56,000-00:42:26,000)
Refusing to join SCLC under King's nonviolence-as-life-philosophy terms (Segments 186-194, 00:46:06,000-00:48:31,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before: 'set' by ninth grade on a football-and-money future, never planning to come further south than Columbus, Ohio. After: SNCC chair, accepting the 'blood price' of the work, telling students that 'the struggle continues.'
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Yes -- McDew's transformation is not romantic or sudden; it is the result of being beaten, arrested three times, and asked by his peers to speak for them. The pragmatic, unillusioned tone is itself inspirational.
transformation_growth.justification: The transformation is concrete, situated in specific events, and described without romanticization. The pivot from 'designated driver' to 'three arrests in two days' to 'spokesperson' to 'chairman' is one of the cleanest arcs in the collection.

peak_moments.description: The Sumter cop beating and McDew's fighting back
peak_moments.stakes: Physical survival; first arrest of his life; broken arm and jaw

peak_moments.description: Confrontation with Dr. King at Shaw over nonviolence as life philosophy
peak_moments.stakes: Whether SNCC would exist as an independent organization or fold into SCLC

peak_moments.description: Pledging collectively to accept being 'beaten, bombed, or killed' for voter registration work
peak_moments.stakes: Their lives; the future of the strategy

conflict_types: Generational conflict with parents over college path
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From comic relief (the social discovery of Orangeburg's young Black women) to indignation (the Sumter beating) to grave resolve (the blood-price pledge).
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Three sharply different kinds of high-stakes conflict are developed: physical (Sumter), strategic (Shaw), and existential (the collective pledge). Each is anchored in specific scenes.

universal_themes: Coming of age through unwanted experience
Refusing to defer to authority that demands humiliation
relatable_struggles: Being far from home in a hostile environment
Choosing between safety (going home) and commitment (staying)
Negotiating coalition with people whose principles differ from yours
human_moments: The teenage 'I thought I was in heaven' on first seeing Black girls in Orangeburg (Segment 115, 00:29:27,000)
His father saying 'come home... you're not going to live' (Segment 159, 00:39:44,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences encountering the interview can connect to the universal experience of having a parent's career plan upended by their child's lived experience, the choice to stand up to humiliating authority, and the negotiation among allies whose principles diverge.
relatable_experiences.justification: McDew's coming-of-age humor and his father's protective worry are broadly relatable. The voter-registration strategy chapter, while historically rich, is more analytic than personal and limits relatability for general audiences.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: McDew's transformation arc is one of the strongest in the collection -- a clean before-and-after rooted in identifiable events, not retrospective myth. Conflict and stakes are vividly drawn. Relatability is moderated by the heavily strategic content of the second half, which is more historian-facing than general-audience-facing.

barriers: Occasional ASR transcription artifacts (e.g., 'Posse Foundation Foundation' doubling, 'snake' for 'SNCC') may confuse readers of the transcript
Movement acronyms (SCLC, NAACP, SNCC) used without expansion in places
language_clarity.justification: McDew is articulate, self-contextualizing, and conversational. Most acronyms are explained somewhere in context. The transcription itself contains artifacts (the 'snake/SNCC' rendering and the 'Posse Foundation Foundation' doubling) that affect text-readers but not audio-listeners.

high_energy_sections: Sumter arrest sequence (Segments 124-156, 00:32:17,000-00:39:09,000) -- continuous escalation with reconstructed dialogue
Shaw founding meeting and Gandhi argument (Segments 184-200, 00:45:17,000-00:50:12,000)
slow_sections: Mid-chapter philosophical elaboration on the strategic rationale for voter registration (Segments 262-294, 01:03:51,000-01:09:21,000) -- substantive but more lecture-paced
Closing reflections on Posse Foundation and education (Segments 311-336, 01:12:13,000-01:18:17,000) -- meditative and discursive
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Around 82 minutes; engaged listeners can follow the full arc, but the middle voter-registration analysis benefits from a listener already oriented to civil-rights history.
narrative_flow.justification: Mosnier sets up clear pivots and explicitly manages pacing ('I'm watching the clock'), and McDew moves through events crisply. The strategic-theory section is intellectually dense and slows the rhythm without becoming inaccessible.

themes_connected: Working-class union culture as preparation for collective political action
The strategic theory of voter registration as structural attack on legislative power
Education as ongoing civil-rights work into the present
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: McDew explicitly connects the founding-era voter-registration strategy to ongoing struggles over voting rights, to the Posse Foundation and HBCU support, and to the need for required civil-rights curricula -- making the contemporary stakes explicit.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Strong. McDew repeatedly tells listeners what to take from each scene, particularly in the closing chapter on education.
broader_themes_connection.justification: McDew is a generous interpreter of his own story, drawing explicit lines from the 1960 founding to contemporary education and voting struggles. The closing meditation on the soul of the nation is one of the more durable big-picture statements in the collection.

accessibility.overall_assessment: McDew is articulate, intellectually rigorous, and self-contextualizing. The interview rewards attentive listening and explicitly frames its contemporary relevance. Transcription artifacts ('snake/SNCC') affect readers more than listeners. The middle strategic-theory section is the densest passage and may benefit from a listener with some civil-rights background.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: Charles F. McDew's interview is among the highest-value primary sources in the Civil Rights History Project. As the second chairman of SNCC, recounting his unusual Northern industrial-union upbringing, his three-arrests-in-two-days political awakening in Sumter, his refusal of Dr. King's terms at Shaw, and his articulation of the Black Belt voter-registration strategy, McDew offers both rich personal narrative and rare insider historiography. The transformation arc is concrete and unromanticized; the strategic analysis is unusually clear; the historical specificity is high. The one consequential factual blemish is McDew's misspoken '1964' for the February 1, 1960 Greensboro sit-in, which the surrounding chronology makes obvious. Engagement is excellent without straying into performance; the closing reflections on education and the continuing struggle ground the testimony in contemporary stakes.
key_strengths: Unmatched insider account of the April 1960 Shaw University meeting that produced SNCC, including the specific reason 'nonviolent' was added to the name
Concrete and unromanticized transformation arc from Northern teenager to SNCC chairman, anchored in specific events (Sumter beating, three arrests, Greensboro)
Unusually clear articulation of the strategic rationale for Black Belt voter registration as an attack on Senate seniority
key_limitations: One factual misspeak: Greensboro sit-in dated 'February 1, 1964' (historically February 1, 1960) -- McDew clearly meant 1960; this is a chronology-evident error rather than a substantive misremembering
Transcription artifacts (the 'snake' rendering of 'SNCC' and 'Posse Foundation Foundation' doubling) affect text-readers and require disambiguation

claims.claim: McDew was the second chairman of SNCC.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 216 implies it ('You soon have to replace, was it at that fall meeting at Atlanta that you replaced Barry as, Marion Barry as the formal chair of, yeah') and the civil_rights_facts.json corpus entry for 'Charles F. "Chuck" McDew' confirms he was the second chairman of SNCC (October 1960 - June 1963), succeeding Marion Barry.
claims.rationale: Both transcript and corpus agree. The transcript exchange about replacing Barry is brief but unambiguous.

claims.claim: McDew was born June 23, 1938 in Massillon, Ohio.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 9 (00:01:13,000): 'I was born in Massillon, Ohio, June 23, 1938.'
claims.rationale: Direct quote from speaker.

claims.claim: McDew's father played for Paul Brown's Massillon Tigers.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 13 (00:02:13,000): 'My father had played for Paul Brown and the Massillon Tigers. My grandfather had played in Massalon for the Massillon Tigers.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support.

claims.claim: McDew's father was trained as a teacher but could not be hired in northern schools before the 1954 Brown v. Board decision.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 27 (00:06:01,000): 'My father was trained to be a teacher. But when he got out of school, there just weren't many if any black teachers in northern schools.' Segment 97 (00:23:24,000): 'They didn't have black teachers in most northern schools until 54 until the Supreme Court decision of Brown vs. Board of Education.'
claims.rationale: Two direct statements in the transcript establish both the training and the pre-1954 hiring barrier.

claims.claim: McDew's uncle Horace Gillom was a punter and end for the Cleveland Browns.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 52 (00:11:07,000): 'The relatives Horace Gillom, who was an end and a punter for the Cleveland Browns, was my uncle.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support; order of 'end and punter' versus 'punter and end' is cosmetic.

claims.claim: McDew attended South Carolina State College in Orangeburg starting fall 1959.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 94 (00:22:21,000): 'I was sent... to South Carolina State College in Orangeburg, South Carolina. That's where he had gone to school.' Segment 100 (00:24:42,000): 'I went to Riven Orchburg, fall of 1959.'
claims.rationale: Both location and date directly stated.

claims.claim: Over Thanksgiving 1959 in Sumter, South Carolina, police beat McDew after he failed to address an officer with the required deference; he was arrested for the first time in his life with charges of disturbing the peace and disobeying an officer; the beating broke his arm and his jaw.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 123-142 (00:31:53,000-00:36:09,000) describe Thanksgiving at his roommate Charles Gatson's home in Sumter, the cop's 'Where are you from, boy?' the officer's 'didn't they ever teach you how to say yes sir, no sort of white men up there?' the fight, the beating, the broken arm and jaw, and the disturbing-the-peace/disobeying-an-officer charges.
claims.rationale: All specific facts cited (date, place, charges, injuries, friend's name) appear in the transcript.

claims.claim: On the same trip, McDew was arrested a second time for refusing to ride in the segregated baggage car and a third time for cutting through Edisto Park; three arrests in roughly two days.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 143-154 (00:36:25,000-00:38:46,000) cover the baggage car refusal, re-arrest, the Edisto Park shortcut, and the third arrest. Segment 154 says 'I've been arrested for the third time in two days.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support for sequence, locations, and count.

claims.claim: After the February 1960 Greensboro sit-in by North Carolina A&T students, fellow students approached McDew and asked him to be their spokesperson; McDew initially refused.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 163 (00:40:38,000): 'February 1, 1964 students from North Carolina A&T, black students, sat down at lunch counter and Greensboro, North Carolina.' Segments 165-172 describe students approaching him and asking him to be spokesperson, and his initial refusal.
claims.rationale: All the relational claims are supported. The date is a partial-support flag: the transcript says February 1, 1964, the historically correct date is February 1, 1960, and the surrounding chronology (he is a freshman in his first semester) makes 1960 obvious. The summary uses 1960. Status marked partially_supported because the summary substitutes a different date than the speaker actually uttered.

claims.claim: McDew became head of the Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement and the student leader of the Orangeburg sit-ins.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 174-176 (00:42:46,000-00:43:26,000): 'I became the head of what we call the Orangeburg Movement for civic improvement. I became the student leader for the student protest in Orangeburg.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support; matches civil_rights_facts.json corpus entry.

claims.claim: McDew was arrested in Columbia, South Carolina after attempting to deliver a petition to Governor Ernest 'Fritz' Hollings; Black people were permitted at the State Capitol only between hours of four and six on Sundays.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 178-181 (00:43:38,000-00:44:30,000): 'As we took a petition to the governor, then Governor Ernest "Fritz" Hollings... when we see Governor Ernest "Fritz" Hollings was arrested, because black people were only allowed to be at the Capitol, like between hours of four and six on Sunday.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support.

claims.claim: Roughly 800 students were arrested in an Orangeburg desegregation petition action.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 182 (00:44:49,000): 'about 800 of us were arrested.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support; also corroborated by the civil_rights_facts.json corpus entry for 'Charles F. McDew - Orangeburg Movement for Civic Improvement' which mentions the 'March 15, 1960 mass arrest action (~800 students).'

claims.claim: In April 1960 McDew attended the Shaw University meeting in Raleigh that produced SNCC, called by Dr. King and the Southern Christian Leadership Conference.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 183-184 (00:44:49,000-00:45:17,000): 'got a letter from Dr. King and Southern Christian Leadership Conference that there would be a meeting of students at Shaw University in Rowling, North Carolina to discuss the student's sit-ins that took place in April 1960.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support; matches civil_rights_facts.json corpus for the founding meeting.

claims.claim: At Shaw, Dr. King proposed that students join SCLC and pledge nonviolence as a way of life; McDew refused on the ground that what worked for Gandhi in India would not protect protesters in the American South.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 186-193 (00:46:06,000-00:48:05,000): 'Dr. King felt we should all join SCLC. I disagreed... Dr. King felt if you join... that you should accept nonviolence as a way of life... I said in the United States, nonviolence won't work... if a group of black people lay down on railroad tracks here in South Carolina, Georgia, Alabama, Mississippi, Texas, Louisiana, any of these southern states a train would run you over a backup to make certain you are dead.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support for all elements of the claim.

claims.claim: Those who refused to pledge nonviolence as a life philosophy convened down the hall and founded SNCC; the word 'nonviolent' was added to the name to include the Nashville students trained by James Lawson.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 194-199 (00:48:05,000-00:50:01,000) describe the down-the-hall meeting and the decision to include 'nonviolent' in the name to keep the Nashville Lawson-trained students in the coalition.
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support.

claims.claim: Marion Barry became SNCC's first chairman; McDew succeeded him.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 211 (00:53:05,000): 'elected Marion. Barry is a first chairman.' Segment 216 (00:55:07,000): 'You soon have to replace, was it at that fall meeting at Atlanta that you replaced Barry as, Marion Barry as the formal chair of, yeah.'
claims.rationale: Both facts directly supported; matches civil_rights_facts.json.

claims.claim: SNCC tested the jail-no-bail tactic in Rock Hill, South Carolina; Rev. C.A. Ivory and I. DeQuincey Newman (head of the South Carolina NAACP) were central local figures.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 220-227 (00:56:03,000-00:58:25,000): 'we talked about a Jail-no-bail program and where that was going to be tried out was Rock Hill, South Carolina... it was Rev. C.A. Ivory, who was very involved in Rock Hill, South Carolina. And the head of the NAACP at the time, guy named I. DeQuincey Newman.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support for all named figures and the jail-no-bail framing.

claims.claim: SNCC's first Mississippi voter-registration project was in McComb, Southwest Mississippi.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 236 (00:59:52,000): 'we went to McComb, Mississippi in Southwest Mississippi, basically to register voters.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support.

claims.claim: SNCC chose Black Belt counties with heavy Black populations and low Black voter registration in order to attack the seniority-based power of long-tenured Southern senators including James Eastland, John Stennis, Robert Byrd, and Herman Talmadge.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 263-282 (01:04:02,000-01:07:09,000) describe the strategic analysis of seniority, the targeting of Black Belt counties, the naming of Dougherty/Terrell/Baker/Amite/Pike/Walthall counties, and the list 'the Stenesis and the James Eastland came from, and the (Robert) Byrd and the (Herman) Talmadge.'
claims.rationale: All elements supported except that Robert Byrd was a senator from West Virginia (a former Klan member but not a representative of the Deep South Black Belt) and his inclusion in this list -- present in the transcript -- may reflect a speaker slip. The summary preserves McDew's list as spoken; status partially_supported because the inclusion of Byrd in the Black Belt seniority example is geographically inaccurate even though the speaker said it.

claims.claim: SNCC members collectively pledged that they would not be turned back by being 'beaten, bombed, or killed'; McDew described the campaign as a 'blood price' the movement had decided to pay.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 299-307 (01:10:09,000-01:11:45,000): 'we have to pledge to each other that when it does happen, whether it be beaten, bombed, or killed, we will not turn away... we had to pledge that we knew there was going to be a blood price that had to be paid, and that we were going to pay it.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support for both the pledge and the 'blood price' framing.

claims.claim: SNCC was planned with a finite five-year horizon, in part because student members had to give their parents a concrete commitment.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 249-261 (01:01:57,000-01:03:32,000): 'we said that it's only gone last for a certain period of time, a finite period of time, like five years... you had to say, I have to do this, this is what I'm going to do... There's going to be a period that I'm going to do this for five years.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support.

claims.claim: McDew spoke at Colby College in Maine to the Posse Foundation.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 314-316 (01:13:04,000-01:13:33,000): 'I was at Colby College in Maine. And Colby has, I see that I was speaking to the Posse Foundation Foundation.'
claims.rationale: The transcript renders the name as 'Posse Foundation Foundation' (apparent ASR doubling or stutter), but the entity is clearly the Posse Foundation.

claims.claim: President Obama directed a portion of his Nobel Peace Prize proceeds to the Posse Foundation.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 324 (01:14:57,000): 'President Obama, when he won the Nobel Peace Prize, gave a considerable amount of that money to the Posse Foundation Foundation.'
claims.rationale: Direct transcript support.

claims.claim: The Children's Defense Fund was founded by Marian Wright Edelman.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 337-338 (01:18:42,000-01:18:51,000): 'I think the work of the children is defensively fun. I'm sorry. The defense fund is wonderful in what it's doing and should be supported.' civil_rights_facts.json confirms Edelman founded the Children's Defense Fund in 1973.
claims.rationale: McDew mentions the Children's Defense Fund but does not name Marian Wright Edelman as its founder in the transcript. The founding attribution is contextual knowledge from the ground-truth corpus, not a transcript-supported claim. Marked partially_supported.

claims.claim: Mississippi public schools require a course in civil rights before college completion.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segment 329 (01:16:20,000): 'in Mississippi, the Mississippi public schools, you have to take a course and pass course and civil rights before you finish college.'
claims.rationale: The summary preserves the speaker's claim verbatim. (This is a claim about Mississippi K-12/college policy that the speaker makes; the citation is to what he says, not to an independent verification.)

claims.claim: The interview was conducted June 4, 2011 in Albany, Georgia at Albany State University by Joseph Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at UNC Chapel Hill, on behalf of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Segments 1-5 (00:00:00,000-00:00:53,000) contain the standardized opening: 'today is Saturday, June 4, 2011. My name is Joe Maneye [sic; Joe Mosnier] of the Southern oral history program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill... we are here to complete an oral history interview for the Civil Rights History Project which is a joint undertaking of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian's National Museum of African American History and Culture. We are in Albany, Georgia today. In fact, on the campus Albany State University.'
claims.rationale: All facts directly stated in the standardized opening. The transcription renders 'Mosnier' as 'Maneye' (ASR error); the canonical interviewer name is Joseph Mosnier.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's accuracy score (84) and quality score (89) both fall below the 90/90 Smithsonian publication threshold, primarily because the summary silently corrects McDew's misspoken Greensboro date from '1964' to '1960' and adds two contextual inferences (Marian Wright Edelman as Children's Defense Fund founder; St. James AME church located in Massillon) that are correct against the ground-truth corpus but not directly stated by the speaker. The citation audit identifies three partially_supported claims (the Greensboro date substitution, Robert Byrd's inclusion in the Black Belt Senate seniority example, and the Edelman attribution). No claims are unsupported; all factual content is either transcript-supported or transcript-supported with a flagged paraphrase widening. The interview is historically exceptional and the summary is substantively accurate, but the fail-closed gate routes this to human review so a Smithsonian/LoC editor can decide whether (a) to keep the historically-correct date substitution with a footnote, (b) to revert to the speaker's spoken date with a [sic] annotation, or (c) to remove the date entirely. Recommended editor action: keep the historically-correct date with a transparent annotation; the other two flagged items are minor contextualizations that an editor can accept or strip.
